notes.note: Theophilus, Marcellus's freedman and courier, carries this short covering note (the same bearer as Fam. 4.9). Cicero writes only because he cannot let the messenger leave for Mytilene empty-handed.

notes.anchor: see to it that you come as soon as may be
notes.note: The briefest and most urgent of the four appeals to the exiled Marcellus (cos. 51): Caesar's pardon has been granted, and Cicero presses him to make use of it without further delay, fearing that his "slow departure" from Mytilene is hardening into permanent reluctance.

notes.anchor: it greatly concerns the state of your household
notes.note: Cicero hints at a practical motive for return: Marcellus's property and finances (his res familiaris) were at risk during his prolonged absence — a danger he had raised more bluntly in Fam. 4.7, warning against "a raid by plunderers" upon the exile's fortunes.

notes.anchor: Only let me know, please, by what date we are to expect you
notes.note: The closing request for a firm date of arrival signals that the matter is now settled in principle and only the timing remains; Marcellus's own reply, Fam. 4.11, confirms that Cicero's letters were decisive in persuading him to accept the recall.
